failure_modes.text: Novice scaffolding can become drag for experts once redundant detail starts adding cognitive load instead of support.
failure_modes.source_quote: The additional material imposes redundant cognitive load.
failure_modes.mitigation: Adjust scaffolding to the learner's actual competence level; novice support becomes drag for experts once it adds redundant cognitive load.

failure_modes.text: Compression can backfire when leaders assume experienced people need less guidance even though the task or context is still novel.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Less Guidance Is Confused with Better Guidance.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify that experienced team members still need full autonomy on this specific task: compression backfires when the context or domain is novel despite their general expertise.

failure_modes.text: One-size-fits-all procedures can degrade performance because the real mismatch is the support level, not the raw capability of the operator.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when performance is dropping because support is mismatched to expertise.
failure_modes.mitigation: Customize support level, not just difficulty level; one-size-fits-all procedures degrade performance because the mismatch is in guidance, not capability.

premortem_questions.text: Who is being slowed by support that has become redundant, and who is still exposed because support was removed too early?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when the same message is landing very differently across experience levels.

premortem_questions.text: What part of this prompt, training, or procedure adds load without adding guidance for the people already fluent in it?
Where are we treating less guidance as better guidance even though the stakes or novelty still require renewed structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger When Less Guidance Is Confused with Better Guidance.

heuristics.text: Retune guidance by expertise level so novices get scaffolding and experts get compression, autonomy, or speed.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when training, prompts, or operating procedures must adapt as mastery rises.

heuristics.text: Strip redundant examples and context only when the task is already clear for the more experienced operator.
heuristics.source_quote: If the problem is clear to the model, avoid using excessive examples.

heuristics.text: Reintroduce structure when the task becomes new, high-stakes, or context-shifted enough that expertise alone will not cover it.
heuristics.source_quote: The model misfires when teams strip away support merely because someone is experienced, even though the task, context, or stakes are new enough to require renewed structure.

curation_notes.summary: Focused on support-level mismatch, redundant-load removal, and expertise-sensitive retuning rather than on generic personalization rhetoric.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor AI-specific prompt recipes beyond the source-backed support-mismatch logic.
open_questions: The source is especially rich on expert-system prompting, but the intervention layer stays general so the model remains reusable across settings.

allies: The raw allies section is strongest on cognitive-load-theory, curse-of-knowledge, and abstraction-fit problems.
antagonists: The source is strongest against redundancy, confirmation bias inside expertise, and static support assumptions.
structured_tensions: There is clean tension between removing drag for experts and preserving enough structure when novelty or stakes return.
